APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This is a clearly relevant NLP resource paper: it presents a manually aligned, sentence-level corpus of three drafts of argumentative college essays with a two-tier revision schema and reported inter-annotator agreement (Kappa 0.84/0.71) and public release details (60 files per draft-pair). The work targets argument mining/discourse and writing-revision analysis and demonstrates standard NLP evaluations (SVM classifiers, unigram and linguistically motivated features, 10-fold participant cross-validation) which the NLP/ML community expects. The paper situates itself against prior NLP revision and argument-mining work (Zhang & Litman, Daxenberger & Gurevych, etc.) and offers experiments that will be useful to the same audience, so it is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP venue.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has an overall logical structure (Introduction, annotation guideline, data release, applications, conclusion) and clearly states its main contribution early — an annotated corpus plus two example applications. However, the provided text contains many repeated and garbled passages (e.g., multiple verbatim repetitions of “Examples include...” and duplicated paragraph fragments), stray LaTeX/formatting notes, and references to Table 1 without the table, which substantially harms readability. Notation is minimal and adequate, but the pervasive formatting and repetition errors make important sections hard to follow and require significant cleanup.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper provides a useful new dataset (three drafts of college-level argumentative essays, sentence-level alignments, and revision-purpose annotations) that fills a gap between prior Wikipedia revision corpora and sentence-level paraphrase/error datasets. However, the annotation schema and alignment/classification methods largely follow prior work (explicitly "follows the two-tiered schema used by Zhang and Litman (2015)" and uses existing alignment algorithms), and the technical contributions are mainly dataset construction and replication/extension rather than novel methods. The inclusion of L1/L2 metadata and multi-draft argumentative focus is a meaningful but incremental advance likely to be valuable rather than surprising to experts.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claim (release of a sentence-aligned, purpose-annotated revision corpus) is adequately supported by the described annotation process and reported inter-annotator agreement (κ=0.84 coarse, 0.71 fine), and the annotation/storage details (Section 3.4) are concrete. However, experimental support for the classification and behavior analyses is under-specified: the excerpt omits dataset size, class distributions, quantitative results and exact statistical tests (Section 4.2), and the H6 training/test splitting procedure is unclear and potentially problematic. The use of number-of-revisions as a proxy for improvement is an acknowledged approximation but is an unsupported assumption without evaluative scores. Overall the technical approach is reasonable but missing critical experimental details and clarity, so conclusions are only partially justified.

IMPACT.justification: The paper fills a clear gap by providing a sentence-aligned, manually labeled corpus of between-draft revisions for argumentative essays ("three drafts of essays... aligned at the sentence level, and the purpose of each revision is manually coded"), which is rare compared to single-draft resources. Because the release includes metadata, reliable annotations (Kappa 0.84/0.71) and explicit demonstrations of use ("Rewriting behavior data analysis" and "Automatic revision purpose classification"), it enables new empirical studies and reproducible classifier development for revision analysis. The resource is likely to be widely reused across argument mining, automated feedback/educational technology, paraphrase/correction detection and computational stylistics ("may also be of interest to research on paraphrase comparisons, grammar error correction, and computational stylistics"), giving it strong practical impact beyond a narrow niche.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant prior work—most notably reproducing Zhang & Litman (2015)’s revision-classification method and using a unigram baseline (Section 4.2.2), and it cites relevant corpora and revision-analysis studies (e.g., Daxenberger & Gurevych, Bronner & Monz, Burstein et al.). Comparisons appear fair: they use cross-validation and report unweighted average F-score, matching the prior-method replication setting. The paper acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably (noting similar improvements to Zhang & Litman and one exception) but provides only a high-level analysis of differences (limited per-category or error analysis), and it omits exploration of some alternative baselines/modern classifiers beyond the SVM+feature set.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper provides useful resources and two applied experiments (student revision behavior analysis and automatic revision-intent classification using SVM and feature groups; see Sections 4.1 and 4.2.2) and reports annotation reliability (κ=0.84/0.71) and data-release details (Section 3.4). However, the experimental results are only summarized qualitatively (e.g., “significantly improves... for half of the classification tasks”) with no detailed tables, metrics, or per-category numbers, and there are no ablation or sensitivity analyses of feature groups or model choices. Related work is reasonably thorough (multiple cited corpora and methods), and some limitations are acknowledged (lack of evaluative data, planned augmentations), but reproducing the classification experiments is hindered by missing feature definitions, hyperparameters, and full result reporting despite describing data splits (10-fold CV, participant splits).
